summary:
The Roman consulate system (established traditionally 509 BC with the founding of the Republic) was the executive magistracy at the apex of Roman Republican governance — two consuls elected annually by the centuriate assembly, holding equal imperium (supreme executive and military command authority) with the right of each to veto the other's actions. The annual rotation and collegial structure were deliberate safeguards against the concentration of power that Romans associated with tyranny after the expulsion of the kings.

The consuls commanded armies in wartime, presided over the Senate and assemblies, conducted the census (until the censorship became a separate office), and gave their names to each year — Roman dates were reckoned by the 'year of the consuls' (e.g., 'in the consulship of...'). The office was initially restricted to patricians; the struggle to open it to plebeians was a central element of the Conflict of the Orders, resolved by the Licinio-Sextian laws (367 BC) which required that one consul be plebeian. The consulate remained the highest regular magistracy throughout the Republic; in extreme emergencies a dictator could be appointed with overriding authority, but for no more than six months. Julius Caesar's acceptance of perpetual dictatorship and then his opponents' accusation that he sought the consulship as a permanent office crystallized the tension between the consulate's annual tradition and his monarchical ambitions.
